title: Lesson 344
transcript: Before we get into talking about all these sorts of fun and interesting recovery strategies like hot spares and cold spares and clustering and redundancy and hot sites versus warm sites and all this kind of fun stuff, there's actually three different failure modes that I first have to define for you here. Essentially, three different ways that equipment can be designed to fail. The first two are really closely related. They're essentially the inverse of each other. Fail soft versus fail secure. What fail soft means is that a system is designed to fail to a less secure state. which is why Failsoft is sometimes referred to as failing open. So a great example would be, say, a firewall. A firewall is designed to normally let through some traffic. If a firewall was designed to fail soft, to fail to a less secure state, then instead of letting some traffic through, it would probably let all traffic through. And that's another example of why we sometimes refer to failing soft as failing open. Just remember that the technical definition, failing soft means failing to a less secure state. And of course the inverse of that is failing secure, where something is designed to fail to a more secure state. So in the example of the firewall, if the firewall was designed to fail secure, it might just block, if it failed, it might block all traffic. And that's why fail secure is sometimes referred to as fail closed. Fail secure means something's designed to fail to a more secure state. So here's a really good question. Which is better from a security perspective? Seems like a super obvious question, right? You obviously wanna fail more secure, but it's not actually that obvious. Because it depends on what you're prioritizing, confidentiality, integrity, or availability. If you're prioritizing confidentiality, integrity, obviously failing secure is the better option. But what if you're prioritizing availability above all else? In that case, failing soft would be the better option. Because if you're prioritizing availability, like here's a great example I think of. I live on Vancouver Island where there is going to be a massive earthquake that's gonna kill everyone. There's gonna be a huge tsunami and it's gonna suck. there's actually a tsunami warning system on the island. The film's cure laughing is because we all live on Vancouver Island. We're all going to die, so that'll be great. Hey, guys? Yes, not me, says John, who lives in Manitoba. Could not be more landlocked, so John will be fine. So he can keep teaching after I'm dead. All right, so... Think about the tsunami warning system. If there's a tsunami about to hit the island, if there's been a massive earthquake and the tsunami's about to hit the island, would you want the firewall in front of that tsunami warning system to fail soft or fail secure? You'd want it to fail soft, right? To prioritize availability above all else. The third failure mode is different, and it's called fail safe. And what it's all about is when a device, and this is primarily related to physical security controls. It's physical security controls that are designed in a way that if they fail, they prioritize the safety of people above all else. So a great example is door locks in a building. In a lot of buildings, the doors will automatically unlock if the fire alarm goes off or someone pulls the fire alarm. Because what are you trying to prioritize above all else? Above all else, the safety of people. And this is a great example of that. If there's a fire, you want to make it as easy as possible for people to get out of the building. and for firefighters to get in to help save people. So doors unlocking in the event of a fire is a great example of a fail-safe mechanism.
